<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Expanders</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:'var(--blue)'}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:'var(--yellow)'}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:'var(--yellow)'}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:'var(--yellow)'}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:'var(--ink)'}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:'var(--yellow)'}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:'var(--mute)'}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Expanders

Sparse graphs that are nevertheless impossible to cut: every small set of vertices touches many new neighbours, the second eigenvalue stays bounded away from the trivial one, and a random walk forgets where it started in $O(\log n)$ steps. Expanders are the workhorse of pseudorandomness — codes, derandomisation, and Reingold's $\mathrm{LOGSPACE}$ algorithm for connectivity all run on them.

blocks.html:
1. Combinatorial expansion

Fix a $d$-regular graph $G=(V,E)$ on $n=|V|$ vertices. For a vertex set $S\subseteq V$ write $N(S)$ for the set of vertices outside $S$ adjacent to something in $S$. We say $G$ is an $(n,d,\varepsilon)$-expander when

$|N(S)| \;\ge\; (1+\varepsilon)\,|S| \qquad \text{for every } S \text{ with } |S|\le n/2.$

Small sets always touch lots of new vertices. The restriction $|S|\le n/2$ is unavoidable: $N(S)\subseteq V\setminus S$ has at most $n-|S|$ elements, so any inequality $|N(S)|\ge(1+\varepsilon)|S|$ forces $|S|\le n/(2+\varepsilon)$. The complement side is symmetric, so this is no real loss.

The slogan: $G$ has no "narrow waist". A balanced cut $V=S\sqcup\bar S$ would have $|N(S)|\le n-|S|=|S|$, which the expansion condition rules out; that is why expanders cannot be cleanly bisected.

Vertex expansion explorer
click to toggle vertices · watch $|N(S)|/|S|$
click vertices to add them to $S$.

Toggle a few vertices on the cycle versus the Petersen graph: on $C_{10}$ a contiguous set of size $k\le 5$ has $|N(S)|=2$ regardless of $k$, so $|N(S)|/|S|\to 0$ — definitely not an expander. The Petersen graph keeps the ratio bounded above by $1$ for every small set; that is what expansion looks like at $n=10$.

Used in

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#exp-svg"), out = $("#exp-out");
  const sel = $("#exp-graph"), btnClear = $("#exp-clear");
  const W = 720, H = 320;
  const LIB = {
    "cycle10": (function(){
      const c=[360,160], r=120, P=[];
      for(let k=0;k<10;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/10; P.push([c[0]+r*Math.cos(a), c[1]+r*Math.sin(a)]); }
      const E=[]; for(let i=0;i<10;i++) E.push([i,(i+1)%10]);
      return {pos:P, edges:E};
    })(),
    "petersen": (function(){
      const c=[360,160], r1=130, r2=58, P=[];
      for(let k=0;k<5;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/5; P.push([c[0]+r1*Math.cos(a), c[1]+r1*Math.sin(a)]); }
      for(let k=0;k<5;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/5; P.push([c[0]+r2*Math.cos(a), c[1]+r2*Math.sin(a)]); }
      const E=[[0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[4,0],[5,7],[7,9],[9,6],[6,8],[8,5],[0,5],[1,6],[2,7],[3,8],[4,9]];
      return {pos:P, edges:E};
    })(),
    "hypercube": (function(){
      const cx=360, cy=160, s=80;
      const corners = [
        [cx-s, cy-s], [cx+s, cy-s], [cx+s, cy+s], [cx-s, cy+s],
        [cx-s+30, cy-s-30], [cx+s+30, cy-s-30], [cx+s+30, cy+s-30], [cx-s+30, cy+s-30]
      ];
      const E=[[0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,0],[4,5],[5,6],[6,7],[7,4],[0,4],[1,5],[2,6],[3,7]];
      return {pos:corners, edges:E};
    })(),
    "k5": (function(){
      const c=[360,160], r=110, P=[];
      for(let k=0;k<5;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/5; P.push([c[0]+r*Math.cos(a), c[1]+r*Math.sin(a)]); }
      const E=[]; for(let i=0;i<5;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<5;j++) E.push([i,j]);
      return {pos:P, edges:E};
    })()
  };
  let state = {key:"petersen", S:new Set()};
  function redraw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = SVG("title"); t.textContent = "Vertex expansion explorer"; svg.appendChild(t);
    const g = LIB[state.key];
    // edges
    for(const [a,b] of g.edges){
      const inA = state.S.has(a), inB = state.S.has(b);
      const cross = inA !== inB;
      const color = cross ? "var(--yellow)" : (inA && inB ? "var(--violet)" : "var(--line)");
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:g.pos[a][0],y1:g.pos[a][1],x2:g.pos[b][0],y2:g.pos[b][1],stroke:color,"stroke-width":1.8}));
    }
    // vertices
    const N = new Set();
    for(const [a,b] of g.edges){
      if(state.S.has(a) && !state.S.has(b)) N.add(b);
      if(state.S.has(b) && !state.S.has(a)) N.add(a);
    }
    for(let i=0;i<g.pos.length;i++){
      const inS = state.S.has(i), inN = N.has(i);
      const fill = inS ? "var(--cyan)" : (inN ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--panel2)");
      const stroke = inS ? "var(--cyan)" : (inN ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--mute)");
      const circ = SVG("circle",{cx:g.pos[i][0],cy:g.pos[i][1],r:14,fill,stroke,"stroke-width":2,"data-i":i,style:"cursor:pointer"});
      circ.addEventListener("click", ()=>{
        if(state.S.has(i)) state.S.delete(i); else state.S.add(i);
        redraw();
      });
      svg.appendChild(circ);
      const txt = SVG("text",{x:g.pos[i][0],y:g.pos[i][1]+4,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill: inS ? "#111" : "var(--ink)","font-weight":600,"pointer-events":"none"});
      txt.textContent = String(i);
      svg.appendChild(txt);
    }
    // legend
    const legY = 18;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:24,cy:legY,r:7,fill:"var(--cyan)",stroke:"var(--cyan)"}));
    let tt = SVG("text",{x:38,y:legY+4,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"}); tt.textContent = "S"; svg.appendChild(tt);
    svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:80,cy:legY,r:7,fill:"var(--yellow)",stroke:"var(--yellow)"}));
    tt = SVG("text",{x:94,y:legY+4,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"}); tt.textContent = "N(S)"; svg.appendChild(tt);
    // readout
    const sN = state.S.size, nN = N.size;
    const ratio = sN > 0 ? (nN / sN).toFixed(3) : "—";
    out.textContent = `|S| = ${sN}    |N(S)| = ${nN}    |N(S)|/|S| = ${ratio}`;
  }
  sel.addEventListener("change", e=>{ state = {key:e.target.value, S:new Set()}; redraw(); });
  btnClear.addEventListener("click", ()=>{ state.S.clear(); redraw(); });
  redraw();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Spectral expansion

Now fix a $d$-regular graph and look at the normalized adjacency $\widehat A = A/d$. Because $G$ is regular, $\widehat A$ has $\mathbf{1}$ as a top eigenvector with eigenvalue $1$; the rest of its spectrum lives in $[-1,1]$. Order the eigenvalues

$1 = \lambda_1 \;\ge\; \lambda_2 \;\ge\; \cdots \;\ge\; \lambda_n \;\ge\; -1.$

The graph is a spectral expander when there is a uniform spectral gap $1-\lambda_2 \ge \delta > 0$ across the family. The largest non-trivial eigenvalue $\lambda_2$ measures how close $G$ is to disconnecting (which would make $\lambda_2 = 1$).

Here is the easy direction of the equivalence with edge expansion. Given $S\subseteq V$ with $|S|\le n/2$, write $\mathbf{1}_S = (|S|/n)\mathbf{1} + v$ with $v\perp\mathbf{1}$. Then

$\langle \mathbf{1}_S, \widehat A \mathbf{1}_S\rangle \;=\; \frac{|S|^2}{n} + \langle v, \widehat A v\rangle \;\le\; \frac{|S|^2}{n} + \lambda_2\,\|v\|^2,$

and the left side counts edges inside $S$ divided by $d$. A short calculation turns this into $|E(S,\bar S)| \ge \tfrac{d}{2}(1-\lambda_2)\,\tfrac{|S|\,|\bar S|}{n}$, so a spectral gap forces edge expansion. (Vertex expansion follows up to constants.)

Spectrum of $\widehat A$
drag the slider · watch $\lambda_2$ collapse for cycles
n

The slider sweeps $n$ (or $k$ for the cube); the dots on the strip $[-1,1]$ are the eigenvalues of $\widehat A$. Watch the cycle: as $n$ grows, $\lambda_2 = \cos(2\pi/n) \to 1$ and the gap closes — confirming cycles are not an expander family. The hypercube has $\lambda_2 = 1 - 2/k$, which also collapses but more slowly. Only families with a $\delta$-uniform gap qualify.

See also
  • Spectral graph theory — Adjacency, degree, and the Laplacian
  • Random walks and mixing — Spectral gap and rapid mixing

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#spec-svg"), out = $("#spec-out");
  const fam = $("#spec-fam"), sz = $("#spec-size"), szLabel = $("#spec-sizeval");
  const W=720, H=220;
  function eigs(family, n){
    if(family === "cycle"){
      const out = [];
      for(let k=0;k<n;k++) out.push(Math.cos(2*Math.PI*k/n));
      return out.sort((a,b)=>b-a);
    }
    if(family === "kn"){
      // adjacency of K_n has eigenvalues n-1 (once) and -1 (n-1 times); normalized by d=n-1
      const out = [1];
      for(let k=1;k<n;k++) out.push(-1/(n-1));
      return out;
    }
    if(family === "hypercube"){
      // Q_k has 2^k vertices, degree k, eigenvalues k - 2j with multiplicity C(k,j); normalize by k
      const k = n; // reinterpret slider as dimension
      const out = [];
      function binom(n,r){ let v=1; for(let i=0;i<r;i++) v *= (n-i)/(i+1); return Math.round(v); }
      for(let j=0;j<=k;j++){
        const lam = (k - 2*j)/k;
        const m = binom(k,j);
        for(let i=0;i<m;i++) out.push(lam);
      }
      return out.sort((a,b)=>b-a);
    }
    return [];
  }
  function redraw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = SVG("title"); t.textContent = "Spectrum of normalized adjacency"; svg.appendChild(t);
    const family = fam.value;
    const raw = parseInt(sz.value, 10);
    let n = raw;
    if(family === "hypercube"){ n = Math.max(2, Math.min(8, raw)); szLabel.textContent = "k = " + n; }
    else { n = raw; szLabel.textContent = "n = " + n; }
    const evs = eigs(family, n);
    // axis
    const xL = 80, xR = W - 60, y0 = H/2 + 10;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:xL,y1:y0,x2:xR,y2:y0,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1.2}));
    for(const v of [-1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1]){
      const x = xL + (v+1)/2 * (xR - xL);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x,y1:y0-5,x2:x,y2:y0+5,stroke:"var(--mute)"}));
      const tx = SVG("text",{x,y:y0+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
      tx.textContent = v.toFixed(1); svg.appendChild(tx);
    }
    // eigenvalue dots
    for(let i=0;i<evs.length;i++){
      const v = evs[i];
      const x = xL + (v+1)/2 * (xR - xL);
      const isTop = i === 0;
      const isSecond = i === 1;
      const color = isTop ? "var(--green)" : (isSecond ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--cyan)");
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:x,cy:y0 - 22 - (i%5)*4,r:5,fill:color,opacity:0.9}));
    }
    // gap arrow
    if(evs.length >= 2){
      const x1 = xL + (evs[0]+1)/2 * (xR-xL);
      const x2 = xL + (evs[1]+1)/2 * (xR-xL);
      const ya = 60;
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x1,y1:ya,x2:x2,y2:ya,stroke:"var(--yellow)","stroke-width":2}));
      const tg = SVG("text",{x:(x1+x2)/2,y:ya-8,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":12,fill:"var(--yellow)","font-weight":600});
      tg.textContent = "gap = " + (evs[0]-evs[1]).toFixed(3);
      svg.appendChild(tg);
    }
    // title
    const ttl = SVG("text",{x:xL,y:30,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    ttl.textContent = `${family === "cycle" ? "C_n" : family === "kn" ? "K_n" : "Q_k"}: λ₁ = ${evs[0].toFixed(4)}, λ₂ = ${evs[1].toFixed(4)}`;
    svg.appendChild(ttl);
    // readout
    out.textContent = `n = ${family === "hypercube" ? Math.pow(2,n) + " (= 2^" + n + ")" : n}    spectral gap 1−λ₂ = ${(1 - evs[1]).toFixed(4)}`;
  }
  fam.addEventListener("change", redraw);
  sz.addEventListener("input", redraw);
  redraw();
})();
</script>
</section>

3. The Cheeger inequality

Define the edge expansion (or Cheeger constant) of $G$ by

$\phi(G) \;=\; \min_{|S|\le n/2} \frac{|E(S, \bar S)|}{d\,|S|}.$

This is the discrete analogue of the isoperimetric ratio $\operatorname{vol}(\partial \Omega)/\operatorname{vol}(\Omega)$ on a Riemannian manifold. Cheeger's classical 1970 theorem bounds the smallest positive Laplace eigenvalue from below by $h(M)^2/4$. The graph version, due to Alon–Milman and Dodziuk, says

$\frac{1}{2}\,\lambda_2(\mathcal{L}) \;\le\; \phi(G) \;\le\; \sqrt{2\,\lambda_2(\mathcal{L})},$

where $\lambda_2(\mathcal{L}) = 1 - \lambda_2(\widehat A)$ is the second eigenvalue of the normalized Laplacian $\mathcal{L} = I - \widehat A$. The lower bound is straightforward; the upper bound is the deeper direction, proved by the spectral sweep: threshold the Fiedler eigenvector $u_2$ at every value, pick the best resulting cut, and that cut achieves the right ratio.

Cheeger ratio visualizer
slide threshold on the Fiedler vector · watch the cut

The dumbbell graph has a tight bottleneck: the Fiedler vector splits the two $K_5$ cleanly with $|E(S,\bar S)|=1$, giving $\phi \approx 1/(5\cdot 5) = 0.04$. Slide the threshold and watch the ratio $|E(S,\bar S)|/(d\,|S|)$ — its minimum across all sweep cuts is essentially the Cheeger upper bound, achieved at the natural split. On the path the optimal cut sits at the middle edge; on the cycle, no cut is good (every cut is balanced and uses two edges).

See also
  • Spectral graph theory — Cheeger's inequality

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#ch-svg"), out = $("#ch-out");
  const gSel = $("#ch-graph"), thr = $("#ch-thresh");
  const W=720, H=280;
  const LIB = {
    "dumbbell": (function(){
      const P = [];
      const cL=[200,140], cR=[520,140], r=70;
      for(let k=0;k<5;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/5; P.push([cL[0]+r*Math.cos(a), cL[1]+r*Math.sin(a)]); }
      for(let k=0;k<5;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/5; P.push([cR[0]+r*Math.cos(a), cR[1]+r*Math.sin(a)]); }
      const E=[];
      for(let i=0;i<5;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<5;j++) E.push([i,j]);
      for(let i=5;i<10;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<10;j++) E.push([i,j]);
      E.push([1,6]); // bridge
      return {pos:P, edges:E};
    })(),
    "path6": (function(){
      const P=[]; for(let i=0;i<6;i++) P.push([100+i*100,140]);
      const E=[]; for(let i=0;i<5;i++) E.push([i,i+1]);
      return {pos:P, edges:E};
    })(),
    "cycle8": (function(){
      const c=[360,140], r=110, P=[];
      for(let k=0;k<8;k++){ const a=-Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*k/8; P.push([c[0]+r*Math.cos(a), c[1]+r*Math.sin(a)]); }
      const E=[]; for(let i=0;i<8;i++) E.push([i,(i+1)%8]);
      return {pos:P, edges:E};
    })()
  };
  // small Jacobi eigensolver for the (regularized) normalized adjacency
  function jacobi(A0){
    const n = A0.length;
    const A = A0.map(r=>r.slice());
    const V = Array.from({length:n},(_,i)=>Array.from({length:n},(_,j)=>i===j?1:0));
    for(let it=0;it<200;it++){
      let p=0,q=1,off=Math.abs(A[0][1]);
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++){ if(Math.abs(A[i][j])>off){off=Math.abs(A[i][j]); p=i; q=j;} }
      if(off<1e-11) break;
      const app=A[p][p], aqq=A[q][q], apq=A[p][q];
      const theta=(aqq-app)/(2*apq);
      const t=(theta>=0?1:-1)/(Math.abs(theta)+Math.sqrt(theta*theta+1));
      const c=1/Math.sqrt(1+t*t), s=t*c;
      A[p][p]=app-t*apq; A[q][q]=aqq+t*apq; A[p][q]=0; A[q][p]=0;
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
        if(i!==p && i!==q){
          const aip=A[i][p], aiq=A[i][q];
          A[i][p]=c*aip-s*aiq; A[p][i]=A[i][p];
          A[i][q]=s*aip+c*aiq; A[q][i]=A[i][q];
        }
      }
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
        const vip=V[i][p], viq=V[i][q];
        V[i][p]=c*vip-s*viq; V[i][q]=s*vip+c*viq;
      }
    }
    const idx = Array.from({length:n},(_,i)=>i).sort((a,b)=>A[a][a]-A[b][b]);
    return {eigs: idx.map(i=>A[i][i]), vecs: idx.map(i=>V.map(row=>row[i]))};
  }
  function redraw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = SVG("title"); t.textContent = "Cheeger ratio visualizer"; svg.appendChild(t);
    const g = LIB[gSel.value];
    const n = g.pos.length;
    const A = Array.from({length:n},()=>Array(n).fill(0));
    const deg = new Array(n).fill(0);
    for(const [a,b] of g.edges){ A[a][b]=1; A[b][a]=1; deg[a]++; deg[b]++; }
    // build symmetric normalized Laplacian L = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}
    const L = Array.from({length:n},()=>Array(n).fill(0));
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=0;j<n;j++){
      if(i===j) L[i][j] = deg[i] > 0 ? 1 : 0;
      else if(deg[i]>0 && deg[j]>0) L[i][j] = -A[i][j]/Math.sqrt(deg[i]*deg[j]);
    }
    const {eigs, vecs} = jacobi(L);
    // Fiedler is second-smallest
    const fiedler = vecs[1];
    // normalize by sqrt(deg) to get the actual Fiedler vector
    const f = fiedler.map((x,i)=> deg[i]>0 ? x/Math.sqrt(deg[i]) : 0);
    // recenter & rescale to [-1,1]
    let mn=Infinity, mx=-Infinity;
    for(const v of f){ if(v<mn) mn=v; if(v>mx) mx=v; }
    const span = Math.max(1e-9, mx - mn);
    const fScaled = f.map(v => 2*(v - mn)/span - 1);
    // threshold cut
    const tval = parseFloat(thr.value);
    const S = fScaled.map(v => v <= tval);
    // count cut edges
    let cut = 0, sIn = 0;
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(S[i]) sIn++;
    for(const [a,b] of g.edges){ if(S[a] !== S[b]) cut++; }
    const dAvg = g.edges.length * 2 / n; // average degree
    const sSize = Math.min(sIn, n - sIn);
    const ratio = sSize > 0 ? cut / (dAvg * sSize) : 0;
    // edges
    for(const [a,b] of g.edges){
      const isCut = S[a] !== S[b];
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:g.pos[a][0],y1:g.pos[a][1],x2:g.pos[b][0],y2:g.pos[b][1],
        stroke: isCut ? "var(--yellow)" : "var(--line)", "stroke-width": isCut ? 2.5 : 1.5}));
    }
    // vertices, colored by Fiedler value
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      const v = fScaled[i];
      const inS = S[i];
      const fill = inS ? "var(--cyan)" : "var(--pink)";
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:g.pos[i][0],cy:g.pos[i][1],r:14,fill,stroke: inS ? "var(--cyan)" : "var(--pink)","stroke-width":2}));
      const tx = SVG("text",{x:g.pos[i][0],y:g.pos[i][1]+4,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"#111","font-weight":700});
      tx.textContent = v.toFixed(2); svg.appendChild(tx);
    }
    // legend
    const tx = SVG("text",{x:20,y:20,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    tx.textContent = "Fiedler value · cyan = below threshold (S)";
    svg.appendChild(tx);
    out.textContent = `threshold = ${tval.toFixed(2)}    |S| = ${sIn}    |E(S, S̄)| = ${cut}    ratio |E(S,S̄)|/(d·|S|) = ${ratio.toFixed(3)}    λ₂(ℒ) = ${eigs[1].toFixed(3)}    √(2λ₂) = ${Math.sqrt(2*Math.max(0,eigs[1])).toFixed(3)}`;
  }
  gSel.addEventListener("change", redraw);
  thr.addEventListener("input", redraw);

})();

4. Explicit constructions

A non-trivial existence proof: a random $d$-regular graph is almost surely an expander (Pinsker, 1973). But pseudorandomness applications need explicit constructions — a deterministic recipe one can write down. There are three pillars.

Margulis–Gabber–Galil (1973–81)

Vertices are $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^2$. Edges are given by the action of eight maps from $SL_2(\mathbb{Z})$:

$T_1\colon (x,y)\mapsto(x,x+y),\quad T_2\colon (x,y)\mapsto(x+y,y),\quad T_3\colon (x,y)\mapsto(x,x+y+1),\quad T_4\colon (x,y)\mapsto(x+y+1,y),$

and their inverses. The spectral gap is uniform in $n$, with the lower bound coming from the Kazhdan property (T) of $SL_3(\mathbb{Z})$. This was the first explicit family; the gap is sub-optimal but everything is elementary group theory.

Lubotzky–Phillips–Sarnak (1988)

Fix primes $p\ne q$ with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$. Vertices of the LPS graph $X^{p,q}$ are elements of $\mathrm{PGL}_2(\mathbb{F}_q)$ (or $\mathrm{PSL}_2$, depending on whether $p$ is a square mod $q$). Edges connect $g$ to $\alpha_i g$ where the $\alpha_i$ are the $p+1$ integer quaternions of norm $p$, projected into $\mathrm{PGL}_2(\mathbb{F}_q)$. The result is a $(p+1)$-regular Cayley graph with the Ramanujan property $|\lambda| \le 2\sqrt p$ — saturating Alon–Boppana.

Zigzag product (Reingold–Vadhan–Wigderson, 2002)

Given a "big" graph $G$ on $N$ vertices with degree $D$ and a "small" graph $H$ on $D$ vertices with degree $d$, the zigzag product $G \,\boxtimes\, H$ has $ND$ vertices, degree $d^2$, and a spectral gap that is essentially the product of the two parent gaps. Iterating this gives constant-degree expanders of arbitrary size from a single small "seed" graph — and the analysis is purely combinatorial. This is the construction behind Reingold's $\mathrm{LOGSPACE}$ algorithm for connectivity.

Zigzag product schematic
click the seed degree · iterate to grow

Each iteration squares the vertex count and keeps the degree at $d^2$ — exactly what is needed to get an infinite family at constant degree without losing the spectral gap. Bumping the seed degree raises the asymptotic gap.

5. Applications

Why does anyone care? Because expanders are the cheapest source of pseudorandomness in computer science. A short tour of where they show up:

Derandomisation: walk on an expander

Suppose a randomised algorithm uses $t$ independent random samples and you want to amplify success probability. Instead of $t \log n$ random bits, take a single random vertex of an expander and walk for $t$ steps — only $\log n + O(t)$ random bits, and the expander Chernoff bound says the empirical mean concentrates as well as for independent samples. This is seed extension.

Error-correcting codes (Sipser–Spielman, 1996)

Take a bipartite expander with $n$ left vertices (bits) and $cn$ right vertices (parity checks); each right vertex requires the bits in its neighbourhood to XOR to zero. Expansion forces low-weight error patterns to fail many checks, so an iterative bit-flipping decoder runs in linear time and corrects a constant fraction of errors. These were the first asymptotically good codes with linear-time encoding and decoding.

MCMC sampling

The mixing time of a reversible Markov chain on $G$ is $O((\log n)/\gamma)$ where $\gamma=1-\lambda_2$ is the spectral gap. Designing chains as random walks on expanders gives $O(\log n)$ mixing and turns hard counting/integration problems into rapid sampling.

Reingold's $\mathrm{LOGSPACE}$ connectivity (2004)

Given an undirected graph $G$ and vertices $s,t$, decide whether they are in the same component. Reingold turns $G$ into an expander by zigzag iteration in logarithmic space, then walks $\Theta(\log n)$ steps from $s$. If $t$ is reachable, the walk visits it w.h.p. The construction proves $\mathrm{SL}=\mathrm{L}$, settling a major question in space complexity.

Mixing on an expander vs. a cycle
step the walk · expander forgets fast

The bars show the distribution $P^t(x_0,\cdot)$ after $t$ steps starting from a fixed vertex; the dashed line is the uniform distribution. On the cycle the distribution stays peaked for $\Theta(n^2)$ steps; on the random 3-regular expander it flattens in $\Theta(\log n)$ — that is the spectral gap turning into the practical "forget where you started" speed.

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#mix-svg"), out = $("#mix-out");
  const stepBtn = $("#mix-step"), resetBtn = $("#mix-reset"), gSel = $("#mix-graph");
  const W=720, H=280;
  let n = 20;
  let P = null;
  let dist = null;
  let t = 0;
  function buildCycle(n){
    const M = Array.from({length:n},()=>Array(n).fill(0));
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){ M[i][(i+1)%n] = 0.5; M[i][(i-1+n)%n] = 0.5; }
    return M;
  }
  function buildExpander(n){
    // random 3-regular via random matching unions; deterministic seed for reproducibility
    const M = Array.from({length:n},()=>Array(n).fill(0));
    function matching(){
      const perm = Array.from({length:n},(_,i)=>i);
      // pseudo-random shuffle
      for(let i=n-1;i>0;i--){
        const j = Math.floor(Math.random()*(i+1));
        [perm[i], perm[j]] = [perm[j], perm[i]];
      }
      const E = [];
      for(let i=0;i<n;i+=2) E.push([perm[i], perm[i+1]]);
      return E;
    }
    for(let r=0;r<3;r++){
      const E = matching();
      for(const [a,b] of E){ if(a!==b){ M[a][b] = 1/3; M[b][a] = 1/3; } }
    }
    // ensure rows sum to 1 (approximately) — fix any degenerate rows
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      let s = 0;
      for(let j=0;j<n;j++) s += M[i][j];
      if(s < 0.01){ M[i][i] = 1; }
      else if(Math.abs(s-1) > 0.01){ for(let j=0;j<n;j++) M[i][j] /= s; }
    }
    return M;
  }
  function reset(){
    n = 20;
    if(gSel.value === "cycle") P = buildCycle(n);
    else P = buildExpander(n);
    dist = new Array(n).fill(0); dist[0] = 1;
    t = 0;
    redraw();
  }
  function step(){
    const next = new Array(n).fill(0);
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=0;j<n;j++) next[j] += dist[i] * P[i][j];
    dist = next;
    t++;
    redraw();
  }
  function redraw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const tt = SVG("title"); tt.textContent = "Mixing comparison"; svg.appendChild(tt);
    const padL = 60, padR = 30, padT = 30, padB = 50;
    const gw = W - padL - padR, gh = H - padT - padB;
    const bw = gw / n;
    const uniform = 1/n;
    const maxBar = Math.max(uniform * 2.5, ...dist);
    // bars
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      const h = (dist[i] / maxBar) * gh;
      const x = padL + i*bw + 1, y = padT + (gh - h);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x,y,width:Math.max(2,bw-2),height:h,fill:i===0?"var(--yellow)":"var(--cyan)",opacity:0.85}));
    }
    // uniform line
    const yU = padT + gh - (uniform/maxBar)*gh;
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:padL,y1:yU,x2:padL+gw,y2:yU,stroke:"var(--violet)","stroke-width":1.5,"stroke-dasharray":"5 4"}));
    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:padL,y1:padT+gh,x2:padL+gw,y2:padT+gh,stroke:"var(--mute)"}));
    const lbl = SVG("text",{x:padL,y:20,"font-size":12,fill:"var(--mute)"});
    lbl.textContent = "P^t(x_0, ·)   (yellow = start vertex; violet dashed = uniform)";
    svg.appendChild(lbl);
    // tv distance
    let tv = 0;
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) tv += Math.abs(dist[i] - uniform);
    tv /= 2;
    out.textContent = `t = ${t}    ‖P^t(x_0, ·) − π‖_TV = ${tv.toFixed(4)}`;
  }
  stepBtn.addEventListener("click", step);
  resetBtn.addEventListener("click", reset);
  gSel.addEventListener("change", reset);
  reset();
})();
</script>

6. Ramanujan graphs and Alon–Boppana

How small can $\lambda_2$ be in a $d$-regular graph family? The honest answer is given by the spectral measure of the $d$-regular infinite tree $T_d$ — the universal cover of every $d$-regular graph. Its spectrum is the interval $[-2\sqrt{d-1},\,2\sqrt{d-1}]$ (Kesten 1959). Lifting to finite quotients:

Alon–Boppana lower bound. For any infinite family of $d$-regular graphs $G_n$ with $|V(G_n)|\to\infty$,

$\liminf_{n\to\infty} \lambda_2(A_{G_n}) \;\ge\; 2\sqrt{d-1}.$

The proof: the universal cover is $T_d$, so the spectrum of $G_n$ converges to that of $T_d$, whose top is $2\sqrt{d-1}$.

Definition. A $d$-regular graph $G$ is Ramanujan when every nontrivial eigenvalue $\lambda$ of its adjacency matrix satisfies $|\lambda| \le 2\sqrt{d-1}$ — i.e. $G$ saturates Alon–Boppana.

The LPS construction proves that infinite families of Ramanujan graphs exist at every degree $p+1$ where $p$ is prime. The depth of the result: it reduces the eigenvalue bound to Deligne's proof of the Ramanujan–Petersson conjecture for weight-$2$ modular forms. Each adjacency eigenvalue is a Hecke eigenvalue $a_p$ of a cusp form, and Deligne's $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt p$ (a corollary of his Weil-conjectures proof) is exactly the Ramanujan bound for the graph. The number-theoretic name is no accident.

Ramanujan eigenvalue check
slide $d$ · compare a graph's $\lambda_2$ to $2\sqrt{d-1}$

The strip is $[-d, d]$; the shaded yellow band is the Ramanujan window $[-2\sqrt{d-1}, 2\sqrt{d-1}]$. A graph is Ramanujan iff every dot except the trivial $\pm d$ falls inside the band. The Petersen graph (eigenvalues $3, 1^{(5)}, -2^{(4)}$) is Ramanujan — $1$ and $-2$ both fit inside $[-2\sqrt 2, 2\sqrt 2]$. The cycle isn't really an expander, so its $\lambda_2 \to d$ as $n\to\infty$ and it overflows the band.

See also
  • Spectral graph theory — Bipartite graphs, expanders, and Ramanujan
  • Representation theory — Characters and orthogonality
  • Characters and orthogonality

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $("#ram-svg"), out = $("#ram-out");
  const gSel = $("#ram-graph");
  const W=720, H=220;
  function jacobi(A0){
    const n = A0.length;
    const A = A0.map(r=>r.slice());
    const V = Array.from({length:n},(_,i)=>Array.from({length:n},(_,j)=>i===j?1:0));
    for(let it=0;it<200;it++){
      let p=0,q=1,off=Math.abs(A[0][1]);
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++){ if(Math.abs(A[i][j])>off){off=Math.abs(A[i][j]); p=i; q=j;} }
      if(off<1e-11) break;
      const app=A[p][p], aqq=A[q][q], apq=A[p][q];
      const theta=(aqq-app)/(2*apq);
      const t=(theta>=0?1:-1)/(Math.abs(theta)+Math.sqrt(theta*theta+1));
      const c=1/Math.sqrt(1+t*t), s=t*c;
      A[p][p]=app-t*apq; A[q][q]=aqq+t*apq; A[p][q]=0; A[q][p]=0;
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
        if(i!==p && i!==q){
          const aip=A[i][p], aiq=A[i][q];
          A[i][p]=c*aip-s*aiq; A[p][i]=A[i][p];
          A[i][q]=s*aip+c*aiq; A[q][i]=A[i][q];
        }
      }
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
        const vip=V[i][p], viq=V[i][q];
        V[i][p]=c*vip-s*viq; V[i][q]=s*vip+c*viq;
      }
    }
    return Array.from({length:n},(_,i)=>A[i][i]).sort((a,b)=>b-a);
  }
  function buildA(name){
    let n, edges;
    if(name === "petersen"){
      n = 10;
      edges = [[0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[4,0],[5,7],[7,9],[9,6],[6,8],[8,5],[0,5],[1,6],[2,7],[3,8],[4,9]];
    }
    else if(name === "cycle"){
      n = 12;
      edges = [];
      for(let i=0;i<12;i++) edges.push([i,(i+1)%12]);
    }
    else if(name === "hypercube4"){
      n = 16;
      edges = [];
      for(let i=0;i<16;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<16;j++){
        const xor = i ^ j;
        if(xor && (xor & (xor-1)) === 0) edges.push([i,j]);
      }
    }
    else { // random 3-regular
      n = 20;
      edges = [];
      // simple ring + matching for guaranteed 3-regularity
      for(let i=0;i<n;i++) edges.push([i,(i+1)%n]);
      // pair opposite vertices
      for(let i=0;i<n/2;i++) edges.push([i, i+n/2]);
    }
    const A = Array.from({length:n},()=>Array(n).fill(0));
    let deg = 0;
    for(const [a,b] of edges){ A[a][b]=1; A[b][a]=1; }
    for(let j=0;j<n;j++) deg += A[0][j];
    return {A, d: deg, n};
  }
  function redraw(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = SVG("title"); t.textContent = "Ramanujan eigenvalue check"; svg.appendChild(t);
    const {A, d, n} = buildA(gSel.value);
    const evs = jacobi(A);
    // axis from -d to d
    const xL = 60, xR = W - 40, y0 = H/2 + 20;
    const xOf = v => xL + (v + d)/(2*d) * (xR - xL);
    const ramR = 2*Math.sqrt(Math.max(1, d - 1));
    // shaded ramanujan band
    svg.appendChild(SVG("rect",{x:xOf(-ramR),y:y0-30,width:xOf(ramR)-xOf(-ramR),height:60,fill:"var(--yellow)",opacity:0.15}));
    // axis line + ticks
    svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:xL,y1:y0,x2:xR,y2:y0,stroke:"var(--mute)","stroke-width":1.5}));
    for(let v=-d; v<=d; v++){
      const x = xOf(v);
      svg.appendChild(SVG("line",{x1:x,y1:y0-4,x2:x,y2:y0+4,stroke:"var(--mute)"}));
      if(v===-d || v===d || v===0){
        const tx = SVG("text",{x,y:y0+22,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--mute)"});
        tx.textContent = String(v); svg.appendChild(tx);
      }
    }
    // band labels
    const lL = SVG("text",{x:xOf(-ramR),y:y0-38,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
    lL.textContent = "−2√(d−1)"; svg.appendChild(lL);
    const lR = SVG("text",{x:xOf(ramR),y:y0-38,"text-anchor":"middle","font-size":11,fill:"var(--yellow)"});
    lR.textContent = "+2√(d−1)"; svg.appendChild(lR);
    // eigenvalue dots
    let lambda2 = -Infinity, isRam = true;
    for(let i=0;i<evs.length;i++){
      const v = evs[i];
      const trivial = (Math.abs(v - d) < 1e-6) || (Math.abs(v + d) < 1e-6 && i === evs.length-1);
      const x = xOf(v);
      const yy = y0 - 4 - (i%6)*5;
      const color = trivial ? "var(--green)" : (Math.abs(v) <= ramR + 1e-6 ? "var(--cyan)" : "var(--pink)");
      if(!trivial && i > 0){ if(v > lambda2) lambda2 = v; if(Math.abs(v) > ramR + 1e-6) isRam = false; }
      svg.appendChild(SVG("circle",{cx:x,cy:yy,r:5,fill:color,opacity:0.92}));
    }
    const verdict = isRam ? "Ramanujan ✓" : "not Ramanujan";
    const tv = SVG("text",{x:20,y:24,"font-size":12,fill: isRam ? "var(--green)" : "var(--pink)","font-weight":600});
    tv.textContent = verdict; svg.appendChild(tv);
    out.textContent = `n = ${n}    d = ${d}    λ₂ = ${lambda2.toFixed(4)}    Ramanujan bound 2√(d−1) = ${ramR.toFixed(4)}    ${isRam ? "all nontrivial eigenvalues lie in the band." : "some eigenvalue exceeds the band."}`;
  }
  gSel.addEventListener("change", redraw);
  redraw();
})();
</script>
</section>

Connections

Optimal expanders at every degree (not just $p+1$) became reality only in 2013–15: Marcus–Spielman–Srivastava produced bipartite Ramanujan graphs of every degree via interlacing polynomials, and the non-bipartite case at all $d\ge 3$ remains open. High-dimensional analogues (simplicial complexes with spectral expansion in every dimension) are an active arena bridging combinatorics and geometry.

  • Spectral graph theory — Bipartite, expanders, Ramanujan — the sibling page where the spectral viewpoint is developed for arbitrary graphs.
  • Random walks and mixing — Spectral gap and rapid mixing — the gap that defines an expander is exactly the gap that bounds mixing time.
  • Representation theory — Characters — the LPS bound on Cayley graph eigenvalues factors through irreducible representations of $\mathrm{PGL}_2$.
  • Modular forms — Hecke action — Deligne's bound on Hecke eigenvalues of cusp forms is the source of the Ramanujan property for LPS graphs.
  • Complexity theory — Reingold's $\mathrm{SL}=\mathrm{L}$ proof, expander codes, and PCP-style derandomisation are downstream applications.
